[Page 1]

Available online at www.sciencedirect.com

ScienceDirect

Molecular architecture of enhancer–promoter Kota Hamamoto1,2 and Takashi Fukaya1,2 Abstract

Temporal and spatial specificity of gene expression is highly regulated through a rich milieu of regulatory DNAs embedded in the genome. Enhancers represent a major class of regulatory DNAs that consist of a cluster of binding sites for sequence-specific transcription factors and are thought to facilitate recruitment of transcription machinery to their target promoters from remote locations. It has been over four decades since the discovery of prototypic simian virus 40 enhancer, yet the nature of enhancer–promoter interaction still remains an outstanding mystery in gene regulation. The aim of this review is to comprehensively overview molecular mechanisms underlying enhancer–promoter interaction including the roles of looping factors, higher-order genome topology, and dynamic clustering of transcription apparatus within a nucleus. We propose that cooperative interplay between ‘looping’ and ‘hub’ permits distal enhancers to specifically and dynamically modulate target gene expression over large distances. Addresses Laboratory of Transcription Dynamics, Research Center for Biological Visualization, Institute for Quantitative Biosciences, The University of Tokyo, Bunkyo-ku, Tokyo, Japan Department of Life Sciences, Graduate School of Arts and Sciences, The University of Tokyo, Bunkyo-ku, Tokyo, Japan Corresponding author: Fukaya, Takashi (tfukaya@iqb.u-tokyo.ac.jp)

Current Opinion in Cell Biology 2022, 74:62–70 This review comes from a themed issue on Cell Nucleus 2022 Edited by Kazuhiro Maeshima and Eran Meshorer For complete overview of the section, please refer the article collection Cell Nucleus 2022 Available online 12 February 2022

HeLa cells. Importantly, this ‘enhancing’ activity of SV40 DNA fragment was not lost even after changing its orientation or distance relative to the b-globin gene. Thus, the term ‘enhancer’ was originally coined to describe a class of regulatory DNAs that stimulate transcription activity of linked genes from remote locations in an orientation-independent manner. Just a few years later, the first eukaryotic enhancers were identified from the intronic regions of immunoglobulin genes [2e6]. Recent high-throughput sequencing methods have enabled systematic identification of enhancers throughout the genome [7,8]. The encyclopedia of DNA elements (ENCODE) project has estimated that 926,535 putative enhancers are present in the human genome [9], implicating that a typical human gene is regulated by approximately 40e50 enhancers. It is becoming increasingly clear that sequence polymorphisms at enhancer regions are a major source of phenotypic variations within a population [10e12]. A growing number of evidence also suggest that enhancer malfunctions are often associated with human diseases such as cancer [13,14]. Recent quantitative imaging, whole-genome sequencing, and structural studies have revealed molecular details of transcription machinery with unprecedented precision. Yet, the question of how enhancers interact with their target genes for transcriptional activation still remains an outstanding mystery in gene regulation. In this review, we summarize recent progress in the field and discuss the nature of enhancerepromoter interaction including emerging roles of ‘transcription hubs.’ We suggest that cooperative interplay between ‘looping’ and ‘hub’ permits distal enhancers to specifically and dynamically modulate target gene expression over distances.

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceb.2022.01.003

Keywords Enhancer, Promoter, Transcription, Looping, Genome topology, Transcription hub.

Introduction It has been over 40 years since the discovery of enhancer DNA by Schaffner et al. [1]. A short DNA segment containing 72-bp repeat sequence of the simian virus 40 (SV40) genome was found to dramatically stimulate transcription activity of linked rabbit b-globin gene in

Enhancer–promoter looping model In a traditional view, enhancers have been thought to physically interact with promoter regions of target genes by looping out intervening sequences. Early evidence for this model came from the electron micrograph analysis of DNA molecules bound with the sequence-specific transcription factor Sp1 [15e17]. It was found that homotypic interaction of Sp1 brings together the SV40 promoter and the distal Sp1-binding sites through the formation of DNA loop structure in vitro. This ‘looping activity’ of Sp1 relies on its oligomerization through the glutamine-rich domains, suggesting that homotypic

[Page 2]

protein interaction helps to organize enhancerepromoter interaction. GATA binding protein 1 (GATA1) is a sequence-specific transcription factor essential for the expression of b-globin in erythroid cells. Both the promoter region and the distal enhancer region of hemoglobin subunit beta gene contain GATA1-binding sites, thereby recruiting GATA1 and its cofactor LIM domain-binding protein 1 (LDB1) (Figure 1a). It was shown that selfinteraction activity of LDB1 is required for long-range (w40 kb) enhancerepromoter interaction at the bglobin locus [18e20]. Furthermore, direct tethering of LDB1 was shown to mediate the formation of forced enhancerepromoter loops and drive transcriptional bursting from linked genes [21,22]. In addition to GATA1, PAX3-bound enhancers are also suggested to use LDB1-dependent looping mechanism in myogenic progenitor cells [23]. More recently, LDB1 was reported to be responsible for mediating interchromosomal interaction and the assembly of a ‘multichromosomal enhancer hub’ during the regulation of olfactory receptor (OR) choice in maturating olfactory sensory neurons (Figure 1b) [24]. In the mouse genome, ORs are encoded by a family of more than 1000 OR genes. Clusters of OR genes and enhancers are distributed across different chromosomes, but they form a single enhancer hub through interchromosomal interaction mediated by LDB1 and a sequence-specific transcription factor LHX2, thereby activating only one chosen OR gene in each cell [24]. These observations are consistent with the idea that self-interaction mechanism of LDB1 can

support the formation of regulatory loops over large distances. Importantly, LDB1-dependent loops are suggested to occur independently of other structural proteins such as Mediator, CCCTC-binding factor (CTCF), and cohesin [23e25]. Similar to LDB1, a ubiquitously expressed DNA-binding protein Yin Yang 1 (YY1) also exhibits self-interaction activity [26]. Depletion of YY1 globally diminishes enhancerepromoter interaction and the level of gene expression [27,28], suggesting that YY1 plays a general role in facilitating regulatory interactions in the mammalian genome. DNA guanine quadruplexes are also implicated to serve as a scaffold of loop formation through the direct recruitment of YY1 [29]. However, a recent study reported that most enhancerepromoter interactions are preserved even after acute depletion of YY1 in mouse embryonic stem (ES) cells [30]. Thus, the degree of YY1’s contribution in transcriptional regulation still remains a matter of debate.

Role of CTCF/cohesin in enhancer–promoter interaction Another potential key mechanism that can link promoters and distal enhancers is a loop formation by a ringshaped Structural Maintenance of Chromosomes (SMC) complex cohesin and a zinc-finger DNA-binding protein CTCF [31]. Cohesin and CTCF are thought to play a central role in organizing high-order genome topology via loop extrusion [32e37]. Whole-genome analysis suggested that cohesin distribution coincides with many of enhancerepromoter interaction sites in mouse ES

Organization of regulatory loops via homotypic protein interactions. (a) Interaction between the locus control region (LCR) enhancer and the promoter region of hemoglobin subunit beta (HBB) gene is mediated by LDB1 self-interaction. (b) Olfactory receptor (OR) genes and OR enhancers are distributed across different chromosomes in the mouse genome. In mature olfactory sensory neurons, only one OR gene is chosen for mono-allelic expression. LDB1 mediates interchromosomal interactions of OR enhancers, and resulting ‘multichromosomal enhancer hub’ specifically associates with the expressed OR gene. LDB1, LIM domain-binding protein 1; GATA1, GATA binding protein 1.

[Page 3]

cells [31]. At the protocadherin a locus, disruption of CTCF/cohesin loop has been shown to diminish enhancerepromoter interaction and the level of gene expression [38e40]. Contrary, however, subsequent studies have reported that acute depletion of cohesin or CTCF only moderately impacts global transcription activities [34,35]. This gives rise to the possibility that physical entrapment of enhancer and promoter regions within a cohesin ring only plays a supplemental role in transcriptional regulation. Supporting this view, recent studies revealed that the level of gene expression is largely uncoupled from the 3D genome organization in Drosophila [41e44]. It was also reported that CTCF is dispensable for cell survival and trans-differentiation of human B cells [45]. Importantly, a recent work by Kubo et al. [46] carefully examined the requirement of CTCF in enhancerepromoter interaction. Consistent with previous studies [34,35], acute depletion of CTCF in mouse ES cells only moderately affected the global level of gene expression. However, there were a fraction of genes whose expression was significantly reduced by the loss of CTCF. Remarkably, many of the downregulated genes contain promoter-proximal CTCF sites, and their cognate enhancers are typically located at distal locations (more than 100 kb away). CRISPR deletion of promoterproximal CTCF sites led to coincidental reduction of long-range regulatory interactions and transcriptional

activities, suggesting that promoter-proximal CTCF sites help genes to find distal enhancers presumably by serving as an anchor point of loop extrusion (Figure 2a). Acute depletion of cohesin was also shown to preferentially impair long-range (>100 kb) promoter-anchored interactions in HeLa cells [47]. Similarly, enhancerproximal CTCF sites are suggested to facilitate searching of target promoters from distal locations [48] (Figure 2b). Consistent with this notion, it was recently reported that the distal zone of polarizing activity regulatory sequence (ZRS) enhancer at the human Sonic Hedgehog locus uses enhancer-proximal CTCF sites to activate its target promoter over a distance of w1 Mb [49]. It is known that homozygous deletion near the ZRS enhancer is associated with acheiropodia, a rare autosomal recessive disorder that results in a lack of formation of the distal extremities [50]. Intriguingly, a whole-genome sequencing method revealed that this acheiropodia-associated deletion precisely maps to the CTCF sites adjacent to the ZRS enhancer [49], suggesting that ‘enhancer tracking’ mechanism fueled by CTCF/cohesin helps to ensure correct expression and function of key patterning genes during animal development (Figure 2b). Supporting this view, it was recently reported that CTCF-null mutation causes misexpression of developmental genes in zebrafish embryos [51] and Drosophila nerve system [52].

Current Opinion in Cell Biology

Regulation of enhancer–promoter interaction by CTCF and cohesin. Cohesin is thought to progressively extrude chromatin loops until it encounters convergent CTCF sites. When CTCF sites are present at promoter-proximal (a) or enhancer-proximal (b) location, cohesin will drive asymmetric loop extrusion. This ‘tracking’ mechanism facilitates distal enhancers to interact with their target promoters, especially when they are separated more than 100 kb. CTCF, CCCTC-binding factor.

[Page 4]

Role of coactivators in enhancer–promoter It is well established that sequence-specific transcription factors further recruit coactivators such as Mediator and bromodomain protein 4 (BRD4) to enhancer regions [31,53]. Mediator is a large multisubunit protein complex that consists of three functionally distinct modules: head; middle; and tail [54e56] (Figure 3a). Subunits at the tail module serve as a major interaction surface for a variety of sequence-specific transcription factors. On the other hand, the head module serves as a docking site of RNA polymerase II (Pol II) and general transcription factors [57e59]. It has long been postulated that Mediator bridges regulatory interaction by physically linking pre-initiation complex (PIC) at the promoter region and sequence-specific transcription factors at the enhancer region (Figure 3a). Depletion of Mediator globally diminishes the level of gene expression [60], supporting its general function in transcriptional regulation. Strikingly, however, recent studies reported that enhancerepromoter interactions are largely preserved even after acute depletion of Mediator or PIC components [60e62], implicating that other factors such as LDB1, YY1, and cohesin/CTCF serve as a key structural regulator (see Conclusions). Similarly, inhibition of BRD4 was shown to globally reduce transcription activities without compromising enhancerepromoter interactions [63]. These observations point to a model in which Mediator and BRD4 facilitate recruitment of Pol II to target genes independently of their proposed function as a molecular bridge. This idea was further

supported by recent quantitative imaging studies. As per cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) studies [57e59], molecular widths of the Mediator-PIC complex are estimated to be w20 nm. If enhancerepromoter interaction is directly and stably bridged by Mediator, then spatial distances between enhancer and promoter regions are expected to be separated only by several ‘tens’ of nm in cells (Figure 3a). However, a number of evidence suggest that enhancers and promoters are typically separated by several ‘hundreds’ of nm during transcriptional activation (Figure 3b) [64e67]. Moreover, recent studies have revealed that key components of transcription machinery including Pol II, Mediator, and BRD4 undergo dynamic clustering to assemble ‘transcriptional condensates’ or ‘transcription hubs’ with a size of w200e300 nm diameter [68e70].

Transcription hub model As per the traditional looping model, it is expected that a single enhancer can activate only one target gene at a given time. However, live-imaging study revealed that a shared enhancer can co-activate two linked genes simultaneously in early Drosophila embryos [71]. Strikingly, co-activation can be seen even when two linked genes are separated by homologous chromosomes [72]. Co-activated genes are typically separated by w200e400 nm [64], suggesting that a shared enhancer is capable of recruiting Pol II onto two linked promoters even in the absence of complete physical proximity. Such a physical interaction-independent mechanism is also seen for enhancers regulating the expression of key developmental genes such as Shh and

Formation of transcription hubs at enhancer regions. (a) Stable enhancer–promoter bridging by Mediator. Sequence-specific transcription factors (TFs) interact with the Mediator tail module and facilitate the assembly of pre-initiation complex at the promoter region of target gene. Structure of human Mediator with pre-initiation complex (EMD-12610) was visualized using UCSF ChimeraX [106]. (b) Formation of transcription hub as an emerging new model of gene regulation. Multivalent protein interactions mediated by intrinsically disordered regions (IDRs) of TFs, coactivators, and Pol II are suggested to facilitate assembly of transcription hubs at enhancer regions. TFIIH, transcription factor II H; Pol II, RNA polymerase II; BRD4, bromodomain protein 4.

[Page 5]

Sox2 in the mouse [66,67]. Consistently, recent highresolution imaging studies suggested that spatial distances between enhancer and promoter regions are not necessarily predictive of transcriptional output [42,73]. Instead, there are several examples where enhancerepromoter distances become even larger when genes are activated [66,74]. Then, what mediates enhancerepromoter communication over large spatial distances? Importantly, many of the transcription factors, coactivators, and Pol II are known to contain intrinsically disordered regions (IDRs) that can mediate liquideliquid phase separation via multivalent protein interaction. For example, YSPTSPS heptapeptide repeats of Pol II C-terminal domain are suggested to mediate condensate formation both in vitro and in vivo [75,76], which is consistent with pioneering super-resolution studies showing that Pol II undergoes dynamic clustering in living cells [77,78]. Similarly, BRD4 and Mediator subunits were reported to exhibit condensation activity in an IDR-dependent manner [68]. Intriguingly, BRD4 and Mediator clusters

are found to colocalize with super-enhancers in cells [68,69], suggesting that enhancers serve as a scaffold for the assembly of ‘transcription hubs’ where key components of transcriptional machinery are locally concentrated to facilitate activation of target genes (Figure 3b). Supporting this view, a number of subsequent studies suggested that sequence-specific transcription factors, elongation factors, other key coactivators, and chromatin itself also undergo dynamic clustering within a nucleus [75e98]. Furthermore, it was recently reported that optogenetic induction of condensate formation can increase a frequency of long-range chromatin interaction [99] and transcription activity in living cells [100,101]. Enhancer RNAs are also suggested to modulate an efficiency of hub formation at the enhancer regions [102,103], adding another layer of complexity to this process. It is important to note that IDRs of transcription factors are also suggested to play a key role in regulating the stability and the specificity of chromatin-binding independently of condensate formation [104,105]. Moreover, it was recently

Cooperative interplay between looping and hubs. Pre-assembled transcription hubs are loaded onto target promoters via transient looping. Increased molecular volume within a loop domain separates enhancer–promoter distance by several hundreds of nm.

[Page 6]

reported that enhancerepromoter interactions are largely preserved even after treating cells with 1,6-hexanediol, an inhibitor for condensate formation [63]. Cleary, future functional studies are needed to fully elucidate diverse roles of IDRs in transcriptional regulation.

Conclusions In this review, we have overviewed molecular basis underlying enhancerepromoter interaction in gene regulation. Although looping model and transcription hub model invoke distinct molecular mechanisms, we envision that these two ideas are not necessarily mutually exclusive. For example, distal enhancer regions of the oncogenic MYC locus are highly enriched with BRD4 and Mediator in human leukemia cells. Although BRD4 and Mediator are both required for a high level of MYC expression, promoter Capture-C analysis revealed that neither inhibition of BRD4/Mediator condensation nor BRD4 degradation disrupts long-range enhancerepromoter interactions [63]. On the other hand, acute depletion of CTCF largely reduced their interaction frequency, suggesting that the MYC enhancers use CTCF/cohesin loops as a structural regulator and BRD4/Mediator hubs to increase local concentration of transcription machineries. As discussed earlier, LDB1 and YY1 are also suggested to play a key structural role in enhancerepromoter interaction. It can be possible that these factors help to load ‘pre-assembled’ transcription hubs at enhancer regions onto target promoters via looping mechanism (Figure 4). Once transcription hubs are loaded onto promoters, looping interactions may not be stably maintained because of an increased molecular volume within a loop domain, explaining the reason why enhancerepromoter distances are typically separated by several hundreds of nm in cells. This model can also explain why transcription hubs are specifically recruited to their target promoters without inducing promiscuous transcriptional activation of other neighboring genes. We anticipate that the advent of quantitative live-imaging technologies will provide fundamentally new insights into dynamic interplay between transcription hubs, 3D genome organization, and enhancerepromoter interaction in gene regulation.

Conflict of interest statement Nothing declared.

This study revealed that a 12-kb deletion associated with Brazilian families with acheiropodia maps to binding sites of CTCF and cohesin near the ZRS enhancer at the SHH locus. Long-range interaction between the SHH promoter and the distal ZRS enhancer is abolished by this mutation.

This study showed that BRD4 degradation or treatment with BET inhibitors globally reduces the level of gene expression. Treatment with 1,6-Hexanediol also exhibits similar effects. Promoter Capture-C analysis revealed that enhancer-promoter interactions are largely unaffected even under these inhibitory conditions.
